examples.translation: She got a new job.
I have a meeting at ten.

naturalMeaning: Same form for men and women: o chefe / a chefe.
Same form for both genders: o colega / a colega. 'Colega de trabalho' = coworker.
'Salário mínimo' (minimum wage) is a constant reference point in Brazilian conversation.
Always plural: 'Estou de férias' = I'm on vacation.

examples.text: Mês que vem eu tiro férias.
examples.translation: Next month I'm taking vacation.

examples.text: Qual é o horário de funcionamento?
examples.translation: What are the opening hours?

examples.text: Amanhã tenho uma entrevista de emprego.
examples.translation: Tomorrow I have a job interview.

naturalMeaning: Same form for both genders: o cliente / a cliente.

examples.translation: The deadline is Friday.
Tomorrow is my day off.

examples.text: Preciso ler o contrato antes de assinar.
examples.translation: I need to read the contract before signing.

naturalMeaning: Also what you say when someone sneezes: 'Saúde!' (bless you), and a common toast.
Feminine: médica. 'Ir ao médico' = to go to the doctor.

examples.translation: I need to book a doctor's appointment.

naturalMeaning: The everyday word; 'medicamento' is the formal/pharmacy term.

examples.translation: Did you take your medicine?

naturalMeaning: Context decides: at the pharmacy it's a prescription, in the kitchen it's a recipe.

examples.translation: Does this medicine require a prescription?

examples.text: Minha consulta é às três da tarde.
examples.translation: My appointment is at three in the afternoon.
I have a sore throat.
I think I have a fever.

naturalMeaning: Brazilians say 'gripado' loosely for any bad cold, not just influenza.

examples.translation: I'm allergic to shrimp.

examples.text: O médico pediu uns exames de sangue.
examples.translation: The doctor ordered some blood tests.

naturalMeaning: Private health insurance; the public system is the SUS.

examples.translation: Do you have health insurance?

naturalMeaning: Often abbreviated to 'PS'.

examples.translation: They took him to the ER.
The rent is due on the fifth.

naturalMeaning: Means both the gated building/complex and the monthly maintenance fee you pay on top of rent.

examples.text: O aluguel é dois mil, fora o condomínio.
examples.translation: The rent is two thousand, not counting the building fee.
I'm looking for a furnished apartment.

naturalMeaning: Feminine: inquilina. The landlord is 'o proprietário' or 'o dono'.

examples.translation: My neighbor makes a lot of noise.

naturalMeaning: As a noun: 'Moro no quinto andar.' As a verb: to walk.

examples.text: O elevador parou no terceiro andar.
examples.translation: The elevator stopped on the third floor.

naturalMeaning: Most Brazilian apartment buildings have a staffed portaria; the doorman is 'o porteiro'.

examples.translation: I left the package at the front desk.

naturalMeaning: 'A conta de luz' is the electricity bill; 'acabou a luz' = the power went out.

examples.text: Acabou a luz no prédio inteiro.
examples.translation: The power went out in the whole building.

naturalMeaning: In apartment listings, 'vaga de garagem' means a parking space.

examples.translation: The move is on Saturday.

naturalMeaning: 'Cartão de crédito' / 'cartão de débito'; at checkout you'll hear 'crédito ou débito?'

examples.translation: I'll pay by card.

naturalMeaning: The dominant way Brazilians transfer money; also used as a verb-ish noun: 'te mando um Pix'.

examples.translation: Can you send me a Pix?

naturalMeaning: A barcode bill you pay via banking app or lottery house; rent, tuition and purchases often come as boletos.

examples.text: Esqueci de pagar o boleto do aluguel.
examples.translation: I forgot to pay the rent slip.

naturalMeaning: To borrow something is 'pedir emprestado'.

examples.text: Vou ver meu saldo no aplicativo.
examples.translation: I'll check my balance in the app.

naturalMeaning: Gender changes the meaning: o caixa = cashier/ATM (caixa eletrônico); a caixa = box.

examples.translation: You can pay over there at the register.
Do you have change for a fifty?

naturalMeaning: 'Imposto de renda' is income tax; declaring it each year is a national ritual.
Paying in monthly installments ('em 3x, em 10x') is standard for almost any purchase in Brazil.

examples.text: Dá pra parcelar em três vezes?
examples.translation: Can I pay in three installments?

naturalMeaning: Plural is 'reais': dez reais. Informally people say 'conto': 'dez conto'.

examples.translation: It costs twenty reais.
The internet went down again.

naturalMeaning: Also the numbered ticket you pull to wait your turn at banks and clinics.

examples.text: Qual é a senha do wi-fi?
examples.translation: What's the wi-fi password?

naturalMeaning: Often shortened to 'app' (pronounced 'api'). Ride-hailing is 'carro de aplicativo'.

examples.translation: Download the bank's app.
Send me a message on WhatsApp.

naturalMeaning: To call someone is 'ligar para alguém'.

examples.translation: I missed a call from you.

examples.text: Alguém tem um carregador de iPhone?
examples.translation: Does anyone have an iPhone charger?

examples.text: A tela do meu celular quebrou.
examples.translation: My phone screen broke.
Is there an outlet around here?

examples.text: Te mando por e-mail ainda hoje.
examples.translation: I'll email it to you later today.

naturalMeaning: 'Documento' or 'documentos' often means your ID: 'Trouxe um documento com foto?'

examples.text: Preciso de um documento com foto.
examples.translation: I need a photo ID.

naturalMeaning: Both your wallet and many official documents: carteira de identidade (ID), carteira de motorista (driver's license), carteira de trabalho (work booklet).

examples.text: Perdi minha carteira com todos os documentos.
examples.translation: I lost my wallet with all my documents.

naturalMeaning: 'Pegar fila' = to end up in a long line; 'furar fila' = to cut in line.

examples.text: A fila do banco tava enorme.
examples.translation: The line at the bank was huge.
My visa expires in March.

naturalMeaning: 'Boa viagem!' = have a good trip. The verb is 'viajar' (with j).

examples.translation: How was the trip?

naturalMeaning: For transport tickets; event tickets are 'ingresso'.

examples.text: Já comprei as passagens pro Rio.
examples.translation: I already bought the tickets to Rio.

naturalMeaning: Informally, calling a person 'mala' means they're annoying.

examples.translation: I haven't packed yet.
My flight was delayed two hours.

examples.text: Tenho uma reserva no nome de Silva.
examples.translation: I have a reservation under the name Silva.

naturalMeaning: You'll be asked for it constantly — pharmacies, online shopping, even supermarkets ('CPF na nota?').

examples.translation: Would you like your CPF on the receipt?

naturalMeaning: A pillar of Brazilian bureaucracy: certified copies, signature authentication, civil records all happen there.

examples.text: Preciso autenticar esse documento no cartório.
examples.translation: I need to get this document certified at the notary office.
What's your address?

examples.text: Fiquei muito feliz com a notícia.
examples.translation: I was really happy about the news.

naturalMeaning: In Brazil 'ficar nervoso' often means getting worked up or angry, not just anxious.

examples.translation: I got nervous in the interview.

naturalMeaning: Can be negative (anxious) or positive (can't wait): 'Tô ansioso pra viagem!'
Also describes lively places: 'A festa tava animada.'

examples.text: Tô animado pro fim de semana.
examples.translation: I'm excited for the weekend.
She got worried about you.

naturalMeaning: In Brazil 'bravo' means angry, not brave (that's 'corajoso').

examples.translation: My mom got mad at me.

naturalMeaning: 'Calma!' as an interjection means 'take it easy / hold on'.

examples.translation: He's really funny.

naturalMeaning: False-friend adjacent: it means likable/friendly, not 'sympathetic' (that's 'compreensivo').

examples.text: A sua amiga é super simpática.
examples.translation: Your friend is super nice.

naturalMeaning: Covers both 'annoying' (pessoa chata) and 'boring/awkward' (filme chato, situação chata). Extremely common.

examples.text: Que chato, o show foi cancelado.
examples.translation: What a bummer, the show got canceled.

naturalMeaning: False-friend adjacent: it means polite, not 'educated' (that's 'instruído' or 'formado').
The famous 'untranslatable' word. 'Estou com saudade de você' = I miss you; 'matar a saudade' = to catch up / make up for missing someone.

examples.translation: I miss you.

naturalMeaning: 'Estar com medo' = to be afraid; 'morrer de medo' = to be terrified.

examples.translation: I'm afraid of flying.

naturalMeaning: Implies an established relationship; casually seeing someone is 'ficar com' or 'sair com'.

examples.text: Eles estão namorando há dois anos.
examples.translation: They've been dating for two years.

examples.text: Tenho um encontro hoje à noite.
examples.translation: I have a date tonight.
Are you single? (to a woman)

naturalMeaning: The verb is 'casar(-se)'; the wedding is 'o casamento'.
'Relacionamento sério' = serious relationship.
Standard sign-off in texts and calls, even between friends: 'Beijo!' or 'Beijos!'

examples.translation: I have to go. Kisses!

naturalMeaning: Common friendly sign-off, especially between men: 'Abraço!' or 'Um abraço!'

examples.translation: Thanks, man. Take care!
She invited me to the party.
The party was great.

naturalMeaning: 'Ir pra balada' = to go clubbing / go out at night.

examples.translation: Wanna go clubbing on Saturday?
Traffic is terrible today.
The bar is on the corner.
It's two blocks from here.

naturalMeaning: 'Ponto de ônibus' = bus stop; 'ponto de táxi' = taxi stand.

examples.translation: I'm getting off at the next stop.

naturalMeaning: The weekly open-air produce market; 'fazer feira' = to do the produce shopping.

examples.text: Compro frutas na feira aos sábados.
examples.translation: I buy fruit at the street market on Saturdays.

naturalMeaning: False-friend adjacent: the everyday meaning is 'gym', not 'academia'.

examples.text: Vou pra academia antes do trabalho.
examples.translation: I go to the gym before work.

naturalMeaning: In Brazil 'o shopping' is the mall itself, not the activity of shopping.

examples.text: A gente se encontra no shopping.
examples.translation: Let's meet at the mall.

examples.text: Esse é o melhor açaí da cidade.
examples.translation: This is the best açaí in town.

naturalMeaning: 'Pior que...' is also a filler meaning 'the thing is...' in casual speech.
After an adjective it intensifies: 'bom demais' = really good. Alone it can mean 'awesome': 'Esse lugar é demais!'

examples.translation: I ate too much at lunch.
I worked a lot this week.
I almost missed the bus.

examples.text: Quer dividir? Metade pra cada um.
examples.translation: Want to split it? Half for each.

translation: to manage to / to be able to / to get
naturalMeaning: The workhorse for 'can' in the sense of succeeding: 'Não consigo abrir' = I can't open it.

examples.translation: I got a discount.
I can't sleep.

naturalMeaning: Takes 'de' before nouns: 'Preciso de ajuda'; no 'de' before verbs: 'Preciso ir.'

examples.translation: I need help with this.
I'll try again tomorrow.

naturalMeaning: 'Deixa!' = never mind / leave it; 'Deixa eu ver' = let me see.

examples.translation: I left the keys at home.
Never mind / forget it.

naturalMeaning: Movement away from here (take); 'trazer' is movement toward here (bring). Also 'how long it takes': 'Leva uma hora.'

examples.translation: I can take you home.
Can you bring another draft beer?
I'm looking for an apartment to rent.

translation: to find; to meet up with
naturalMeaning: 'Encontrar com alguém' = to meet up with someone.

examples.translation: I didn't find my keys.
Let's meet at eight.

naturalMeaning: One verb for waiting, hoping and expecting; context decides. 'Espera aí!' = hold on!

examples.translation: Hold on, I'm coming.
I hope it works out.

naturalMeaning: 'Mudar de casa' or 'se mudar' = to move to a new home.

examples.text: Vou me mudar pro Rio em janeiro.
examples.translation: I'm moving to Rio in January.

translation: to schedule / to book; to arrange to meet
naturalMeaning: The go-to verb for setting appointments and plans: marcar consulta, marcar um horário, 'vamos marcar!'

examples.translation: I'd like to book an appointment.

examples.text: Vamos marcar alguma coisa esse fim de semana.
examples.translation: Let's set something up this weekend.

translation: to agree on / to arrange; to match
naturalMeaning: 'Combinado!' = deal!/agreed! Also 'combinar com' = to go well together (clothes, colors).

examples.text: A gente combina os detalhes depois.
examples.translation: We'll sort out the details later.
I rented a car for the holiday.

naturalMeaning: Also 'to subscribe to' a service: assinar um streaming.

examples.translation: Sign here, please.

naturalMeaning: False-friend adjacent: it does not mean 'to attend' (that's 'comparecer' or 'ir a').

examples.translation: He didn't answer the phone.

naturalMeaning: In casual speech 'mandar' is more common: 'te mando depois'.

examples.translation: I sent the document by email.

naturalMeaning: 'Recebi hoje' often means 'I got paid today.'

examples.translation: Did you get my message?

translation: to solve / to sort out; to decide
naturalMeaning: Brazilians 'resolvem coisas' all day: sort out errands, fix problems. 'Resolvi viajar' = I decided to travel.

examples.text: Preciso resolver umas coisas no banco.
examples.translation: I need to sort some things out at the bank.

translation: to take long / to be late

examples.translation: Sorry, did I take too long?
The bus is taking forever.
What happened?

naturalMeaning: Usually with 'de': 'lembrar de alguma coisa'. 'Me lembra de...' = remind me to...

examples.translation: I don't remember his name.
I completely forgot about the meeting.
The movie is about to start.

translation: to finish / to end; to break up
naturalMeaning: 'Eles terminaram' usually means the couple broke up.

examples.translation: I finish work at six.
She broke up with her boyfriend.

translation: to lose; to miss (bus, flight, event)
naturalMeaning: One verb for both losing things and missing transport/events: 'perdi o voo'.

examples.text: Perdi o ônibus por um minuto.
examples.translation: I missed the bus by one minute.

translation: to take / to grab / to catch
naturalMeaning: All-purpose grab verb: pegar um ônibus (take a bus), pegar uma gripe (catch the flu), pegar sol (sunbathe).

examples.translation: I'll get an Uber.

naturalMeaning: False friend: it does NOT mean 'to pretend' (that's 'fingir').

examples.text: Pretendo morar no Brasil um ano.
examples.translation: I intend to live in Brazil for a year.

naturalMeaning: False friend: it means any relative, NOT 'parent' (parents are 'os pais').

examples.translation: I have relatives in Minas.

naturalMeaning: Watch the doors: PUXE means PULL (not push!); push is 'empurre'. Also 'puxar assunto' = to strike up a conversation.

examples.text: Na porta tá escrito 'puxe', não empurra.
examples.translation: The door says 'pull', don't push.

naturalMeaning: False friend: it means 'to watch' (TV, a game), not 'to assist' (that's 'ajudar').

examples.text: Vamos assistir ao jogo no bar?
examples.translation: Shall we watch the game at the bar?

naturalMeaning: False friend: it means weird, NOT 'exquisite' (that's 'requintado').

examples.text: Esse suco tá com um gosto esquisito.
examples.translation: This juice tastes weird.
